nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral reefs and coral rubble areas with poor to moderate coral growth, commonly in shallow coastal waters and lagoons.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a wide variety of foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, copepods, marine pellets, and small crustaceans. Generally advised to offer both carnivore and herbivore preparations, fed in small amounts several times per day.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 3270x2180. Sharp lateral in-situ shot of a live adult showing the diagnostic yellow lyre-shaped harptail with trailing filaments and the greenish head grading to purplish-yellow body; best overall representative image.

Diego Delso, 2704x1803. Crisp full-body lateral view of a live adult, clearly showing the elongate blenny body, long dorsal fin and yellow lyre-shaped caudal fin; different pose from the recommended pick.
Emanuele Santarelli, 2048x1536. Near top-down in-situ shot showing the fully spread yellow harptail filaments and dark body; adds an alternate angle. Colours slightly muted vs the recommended pick.

summary: The Harptail Blenny is a peaceful, reef-safe fang blenny from the coastal waters of East Africa, generally considered a good choice for beginner saltwater keepers. Like other members of the Meiacanthus genus, it possesses mild venomous fangs used defensively, and tends to claim a favorite crevice in the rockwork as its home base.
careNotes:
The Harptail Blenny is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly saltwater blennies, commonly described as hardy and disease-resistant. It tends to claim a specific crevice or hiding spot in the rockwork as its home territory and will retreat there when startled. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised, as the species has been reported to have considerable jumping ability.

Although peaceful toward most tankmates, it has been known to show aggression toward other blennies, similarly shaped gobies, and dartfish — particularly in smaller tanks. Best kept singly or as a mated pair in reef systems. Generally considered reef-safe with no documented tendencies to nip corals or motile invertebrates.

This species possesses mild venomous fangs used defensively rather than offensively — bites directed at predators have been reported to cause the predator to quickly release the fish unharmed. Direct handling risks a defensive bite; hand-feeding is not recommended.

Wild occurrence data suggests this species may naturally prefer slightly warmer water than some retail care guides indicate.

The species' active swimming habit and territorial nature make adequate aquarium volume important; a tank of at least 120 L is generally advised.

Juveniles have been reported to display duller coloration than adults, which develop a striking yellowish hue with dark banding along the body.
breedingNotes: Spawning in captivity has been documented, and the species has been produced commercially by aquaculture operations — captive-bred individuals are occasionally available in the trade. Larvae are planktonic.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4 in / ~10.2 cm) and minimum tank size (24 gal). Did not provide temperature, pH, or salinity — those fields cross-checked against FishBase and additional secondary sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (10.0 cm TL), temperature (26.3–28.9°C from wild occurrence data, 262 cells), taxonomy (Blenniidae / Blenniiformes), native range (Western Indian Ocean: East Africa, Comoro Islands, Madagascar), and breeding mode (oviparous, demersal adhesive eggs). Temperature disagreed notably with retail listings (22.2–25.6°C); averaged across 4 sources gives 24.0–27.0°C.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Third-source cross-check: temperature 74–80°F (23.3–26.7°C), pH 8.0–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, adult size 3–4 in, minimum tank 30 gal. Used in temperature and size averaging.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been documented, including production at commercial aquaculture facilities. Spawning involves distinct pairing, and the eggs are demersal (laid on a surface rather than scattered into the water column) and adhesive. Larvae are planktonic and likely require live small zooplankton as a first food.
